gross.description: Annie Ant walks: Take 5 big steps forward and say each number out loud as you step — 1, 2, 3, 4, 5!

fine.description: Annie Ant dots: Use a crayon to make 5 dots on paper, then point to each dot one at a time and count out loud.

hints: Every time you take one step, say one number. One step is 1, two steps is 2 — all the way to 5!
Hold up your fingers to help you count. When you have 5 fingers up, you made it to 5!
Uncle Ryan says: Say the numbers nice and slow while you step. 1... 2... 3... 4... 5! You can do it!

gross.description: Ellie Elephant jumps: Do 10 small jumps in place and say a number out loud each time you land — 1 all the way to 10!

fine.description: Ellie Elephant draws: Draw 10 small circles in a row on your paper, then touch each one with your finger and count them out loud.

hints: Jump and say a number each time your feet hit the ground. Keep going until you get to 10!
Touch each circle on your paper one at a time. Say the next number each time you touch a new one.
Aunt Kira says: If you forget where you stopped, just start over from 1. Starting over is totally okay!

gross.description: Gary Giraffe shows: When you see a number, hold up that many fingers as fast as you can — see the number 3, show 3 fingers!

fine.description: Gary Giraffe traces: Write the numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 on paper — trace each one with your finger first, then write it with a crayon.

hints: Look at the shape of each number. A 1 is just one tall line. A 2 has a curve and a flat bottom.
When you see a number, hold up the same amount of fingers. That helps your brain remember what it means!
Uncle Ryan says: Trace the number in the air with your pointer finger and say its name out loud. That is the best way to learn it!

gross.description: Oliver Owl claps: When you see a number, clap that many times and count out loud — see a 7, clap 7 times!

fine.description: Oliver Owl writes: Write the numbers 6, 7, 8, 9, and 10 on paper one at a time — say each number out loud as you write it.

hints: A 6 has a circle at the bottom. A 9 has a circle at the top. Look for where the circle is to tell them apart!
Clap and say the number at the same time. Your hands and your voice working together helps your brain remember!
Aunt Kira says: An 8 looks like two circles sitting on top of each other. Once you see that, you will always know which one is 8!

gross.description: Pete Penguin taps: Hold up both hands and tap each finger one at a time while counting out loud — say one number for each finger, and only touch each finger once!

fine.description: Pete Penguin points: Draw 6 small fish on paper, then touch each fish one time with your finger while you count them out loud — do not touch any fish two times!

hints: Say one number each time you touch one thing. Only say a new number when you touch a new one!
Move your finger slowly from fish to fish. Touch the next fish only when you say the next number.
Uncle Ryan says: When you touch each one only one time, you are counting the right way. Slow and careful is the best way!

gross.description: Hazel Hedgehog rolls: Stretch your arms out wide and spin your whole body around in one big slow circle for 10 seconds — round and round with no corners, just like Hazel rolling through the meadow!

fine.description: Hazel Hedgehog spines: Use a crayon to draw a circle on paper, then draw tiny little lines all the way around the outside edge — just like the spines on Hazel's back!

hints: A circle is round all the way around. It has no corners and no flat sides — just one smooth curvy line!
When you spin around, the path your body makes is a circle shape. Round and round — that is a circle!
Aunt Kira says: Start your crayon at the top and go all the way around until the line meets back where it started. No pointy corners anywhere means you drew a circle!

gross.description: Daisy Duck waddles: Walk forward 3 steps, turn right, walk 3 steps, turn right, walk 3 steps, turn right, walk 3 steps — you just walked the shape of a square!

fine.description: Daisy Duck draws: Use a crayon to draw a square on paper — 4 sides that are all the same size and 4 corners.

hints: A square has 4 sides and 4 corners. All 4 sides are the same size — not long, not short, all the same!
When you walk the square, count your turns. You turn 4 times because a square has 4 corners!
Uncle Ryan says: Draw one side going across, then one side going down, then one going back across, then one going back up. Four sides and you have a square!

gross.description: Bella Bee buzzes: Put both arms up over your head and press your fingertips together to make a pointy triangle shape — like a pointy roof on a house!

fine.description: Bella Bee draws: Use a crayon to draw a triangle on paper — make 3 lines that connect at 3 pointy corners.

hints: A triangle has 3 sides and 3 corners. Count the pointy corners when you see one — if you count 3, it is a triangle!
When you put your hands above your head and touch your fingertips together, that pointy shape is a triangle!
Aunt Kira says: Draw one line going down to the left, one going down to the right, then one going across the bottom to close it up. Three lines make a triangle!

gross.description: Carl Crab walks sideways: Walk sideways 5 steps, turn, walk sideways 2 steps, turn, walk sideways 5 steps, turn, walk sideways 2 steps — you just walked a rectangle!

fine.description: Carl Crab draws: Use a crayon to draw a rectangle on paper — make 2 long sides and 2 short sides with 4 corners.

hints: A rectangle has 4 sides and 4 corners. But two sides are long and two sides are short — they are not all the same!
A rectangle looks like a square that got stretched out long on two sides. The long sides match each other and the short sides match each other.
Uncle Ryan says: Draw a long line, then a short line going down, then a long line going back, then a short line going up to close it. That is your rectangle!

gross.description: Kiki Kangaroo moves: Clap your hands then stomp one foot — clap, stomp, clap, stomp! Do it 4 times in a row without stopping.

fine.description: Kiki Kangaroo makes a pattern: Tear paper into small pieces and color some red and some blue with crayons. Lay them in a row: red, blue, red, blue, red, blue.

hints: A pattern does the same thing over and over. Clap, stomp, clap, stomp — it keeps going the same way each time!
Say the moves while you do them out loud: clap, stomp, clap, stomp. Saying it helps your body stay in the pattern!
Aunt Kira says: Clap first, stomp second — every single time. If you mix it up, just start over from the clap!

stickers.target_concept: Sort by size: big vs. small

gross.description: Lila Ladybug grows and shrinks: Stretch your whole body up as BIG as you can for 3 seconds, then curl up as SMALL as you can for 3 seconds. Do that two times!

fine.description: Lila Ladybug sorts: Tear paper into some big pieces and some small pieces. Put all the big pieces in one pile and all the small pieces in a different pile.

hints: Big things take up more room and small things take up less room. Look at two pieces and ask: which one is bigger?
Put all the big pieces together in one pile and all the little pieces together in another pile. Two groups — big and small!
Uncle Ryan says: Hold up two pieces at the same time. The one that covers more of your hand is the bigger one. That is how you can always tell!

gross.description: Ollie Octopus adds: Hold up 1 finger on one hand. Then hold up 2 fingers on the other hand. Bring both hands together and count ALL your fingers — how many do you have?

fine.description: Ollie Octopus draws: Draw 1 dot on the left side of your paper and 2 dots on the right side. Now point to every single dot and count them all together.

hints: 1 and 2 go together to make 3. Hold up 1 finger, then 2 more fingers — count every finger you see!
Draw 1 dot, then draw 2 more dots right next to them. Now count every single dot. You will get to 3!
Aunt Kira says: Adding means putting two groups together. Put your 1 finger group and your 2 finger group together and count them all. That is what adding is!

gross.description: Pip Panda taps: Tap your left knee 2 times, then tap your right knee 2 more times. Count every single tap — how many taps was that all together?

fine.description: Pip Panda draws: Draw 2 circles on the left side of your paper and 2 more circles on the right side. Count every circle — how many do you have in all?

hints: 2 and 2 go together to make 4. Tap your left knee 2 times and your right knee 2 times, then count all the taps!
You can also do 1 and 3 to make 4. Try tapping 1 time then 3 times — count them all and you still get 4!
Uncle Ryan says: When you add, you put two groups together to make one big group. Count all of them together at the end and you will get 4!

gross.description: Remy Rhino stomps: Stomp 2 times, then stomp 3 more times. Count every stomp out loud — how many stomps was that all together?

fine.description: Remy Rhino draws: Draw 2 stars on one side of your paper and 3 stars on the other side. Touch every star and count them all — how many stars do you have?

hints: 2 stomps and 3 more stomps makes 5 stomps in all. Count out loud every time your foot goes down!
Look at your stars. Touch each one and count: 1, 2 on this side... then keep going: 3, 4, 5 on the other side!
Aunt Kira says: Hold up 2 fingers on one hand and 3 fingers on the other hand. Count every finger and you will always get 5. That is the magic of 2 and 3!

gross.description: Hugo Hippo stomps: Stomp 2 times for the small group, then stomp 5 times for the big group. Count every stomp — which group made you stomp more times? That is the group with more!

fine.description: Hugo Hippo draws: Draw 3 dots inside one circle on your paper and 6 dots inside another circle. Count each group and point to the circle that has more dots.

hints: Count both groups. The group with the bigger number has more. The group with the smaller number has fewer!
Draw a line from one dot in the first group to one dot in the second group. If one group still has dots left with no lines, that group has more!
Uncle Ryan says: Count the first group and hold up that many fingers. Then count the second group. The group that needs more fingers is the one with more!
